places.name: FinancialData

transitions.name: Replenish raw inventory
Produce finished goods
Customer demand arrives

differentialEquations.name: Economic profit response dynamics

scenarios.name: Baseline
scenarios.description: Normal demand with all six operational decision parameters exposed for optimization. Defaults are deliberately near, but not at, the profit optimum.

scenarios.name: Demand surge
scenarios.description: Higher market demand. The same six decision parameters remain tunable, but the demand multiplier shifts the true optimum upward for production, inventory policy, price, expedite use, and marketing.

metrics.name: Profit rate
metrics.description: Instantaneous deterministic profit objective. It is a concave quadratic response surface in the six operational parameters, with correlation terms between production/reorder/batch, price/marketing, and expedite/inventory policy.

metrics.name: Cumulative profit
metrics.description: Integral of the profit rate over simulated time. Use this as the optimization objective for longer runs once the FinancialData state has settled.

metrics.name: Negative profit loss
metrics.description: Negative of the profit rate. This is convex, so use it when an optimizer expects a minimization objective rather than maximizing concave profit.

metrics.name: Service level
metrics.description: Fraction of realized customer outcomes that were sold rather than lost to stockout.

metrics.name: Token-count profit
metrics.description: Parameter-aware accounting profit from observed token counts. SoldOrders use the current selling_price mirrored in FinancialData, while production_rate, marketing_spend, and expedite_fraction contribute explicit policy costs.

metrics.name: Profit
metrics.description: Economic profit using observed token counts and the current tunable parameters mirrored into FinancialData: selling price drives realized revenue, production rate carries a capacity cost, marketing spend is charged directly, expedited shipping adds a premium, and inventory/backlog/lost-sales costs remain token-count based.
